title: Basic INSERT Examples
code:
-- Single row INSERT
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, salary, department)
VALUES (101, 'John Smith', 50000, 'IT');

-- Multiple rows in one INSERT
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, salary, department)
VALUES 
 (102, 'Mary Jane', 45000, 'HR'),
 (103, 'Bob Wilson', 55000, 'Finance'),
 (104, 'Alice Brown', 48000, 'IT');

-- INSERT with DEFAULT
INSERT INTO products (product_id, name, price, status)
VALUES (201, 'New Product', 999, DEFAULT);

-- INSERT with NULL
INSERT INTO employees (emp_id, name, salary, manager_id)
VALUES (105, 'New Emp', 40000, NULL);
explanation: Shows single row, multiple rows, DEFAULT and NULL value insertion

complexity.time: O(1) for single, O(n) for multiple rows

title: INSERT SELECT - Copy Data Between Tables
description: Inserting data from one table to another
code:
-- Create backup table first
CREATE TABLE employees_backup AS 
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE 1=0;

-- Copy all IT employees to backup
INSERT INTO employees_backup
SELECT * FROM employees 
WHERE department = 'IT';

-- Copy with specific columns
INSERT INTO employee_summary (dept, total_emp, avg_sal)
SELECT department, COUNT(*), AVG(salary)
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
explanation: INSERT SELECT combines INSERT and SELECT to copy data efficiently

complexity.time: O(n) where n is rows copied

title: UPDATE Statement Examples
code:
-- Basic UPDATE with WHERE
UPDATE employees
SET salary = 55000
WHERE emp_id = 101;

-- Update multiple columns
UPDATE employees
SET salary = 60000, department = 'Sr. IT'
WHERE emp_id = 101;

-- Update with expression (10% raise)
UPDATE employees
SET salary = salary * 1.10
WHERE department = 'IT';

-- UPDATE with subquery
UPDATE employees
SET salary = (
 SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees
)
WHERE performance_rating = 'Average';
explanation: UPDATE modifies existing data; WHERE is crucial to target specific rows

complexity.time: O(n) where n is rows updated

title: DELETE Statement Examples
description: Various DELETE operations with WHERE conditions
explanation: DELETE removes rows; without WHERE, all rows are deleted

complexity.time: O(n) where n is rows deleted

title: Transaction Control Example
description: COMMIT and ROLLBACK demonstration with banking transaction
code:
-- Start transaction
BEGIN TRANSACTION;

-- Debit from account A001
UPDATE accounts
SET balance = balance - 5000
WHERE account_no = 'A001';

-- Credit to account A002
UPDATE accounts
SET balance = balance + 5000
WHERE account_no = 'A002';

-- Verify the transaction
SELECT * FROM accounts
WHERE account_no IN ('A001', 'A002');

-- Commit if successful, rollback if error
-- COMMIT; -- Uncomment to make permanent
-- ROLLBACK; -- Uncomment to undo changes
explanation: Transactions ensure data integrity; COMMIT makes changes permanent, ROLLBACK reverts

title: TRUNCATE vs DELETE Comparison
description: Demonstrating differences between TRUNCATE and DELETE
code:
-- DELETE - removes rows, can be rolled back, logged
DELETE FROM log_table;
-- This can be rolled back before COMMIT

-- TRUNCATE - removes all pages, usually cannot rollback
TRUNCATE TABLE log_table;

-- Difference in behavior:
-- DELETE: Removes rows one by one, triggers triggers
-- TRUNCATE: Deallocates data pages, resets identity counter

-- Note: Some databases allow TRUNCATE to be rolled back
-- in same transaction
explanation: TRUNCATE is faster but less flexible than DELETE
